speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How come Day and others become millionaires from mining stock. How a contractor makes good when he hits Spokane. A black North Carolinan who makes good in Brewster, Washington. How two wives fool their husbands in a brothel. How do you feel about the first thing I want to ask you about?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The first thing I want to ask you about is Joe Wells. You know the Negro, Joe Wells.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, are you from the same Wells family that came out to Deary and lived out there? How are you all related to them?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: The others come out here and they run a blacksmith shop with their son on the Deary you've heard of that. They're different cousins of mine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So did you know Joe Wells?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: The nigger?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Why yes his daddy to raised him.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: I knew his daddy in North Carolina and he raised him. The boys moved everyone to come west and the old man had quite a little land. He raised Joe as a slave and he went through some of those lands and he went and divided his money with the three of them. He said Joe helped make it just as well as his own two sons and here they all come to Idaho.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he Joe's slave before the Civil War? Was Joe his slave before the Civil War?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: He was his old man's slave during the war and so forth. Now in his field, you stay that long with him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he this man's slave or was he his father's slave?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: I don't know so much about that but then he was a slave and he was raised by the old man you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he stayed on and he became like one of the family?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Well anyhow he adopted the name as Wells you know. Just by his own idea.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he treated Joe pretty good?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Yeah, like his own two boys. Now you'd find it quick to business up there in Deary. The Blacksmiths many years and so forth. Well they knew Bob West. There was some more in our country. There were several who lived here at the time. So Bob West wanted him to come up. I wanted to see him. Bob West didn't know what had happened on the mine but they didn't want to make it. He didn't want to come up any of it. He'd talk to him all night. Next day he had breakfast where they told him where he'd come from. He wanted him to be there staying in his home. But the other wanted him to come to town and bring him money to put it in the bank. And he wanted him to be with him. So he'd come down with over ten thousand dollars in gold and put it in the bank.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now who had the money to put in the bank? Bob West or the Wells boys?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Wells boys.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: So then they went and sold out there. And then of course where that place first, where the tunnel goes through, later over here going to Moscarino. Yeah. Before that hill was at the first house right there. There were forty acres. They both left there and lived there until the death before the death. They had to move to another place to take care of them.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: They had to pay them well for taking care of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they had a good blacksmith business up there in Deary? Did they?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: And nobody else knew about that. They were showing to me later.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Well now it says, you know this Wells gets in trouble by the other couple of miles.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: He says, how are you fixing your mouth?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: He says, I don't need anything else.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: They left it to their sister, you know. They had a sister. And so she got to the Wells, you know. At least she was all the way past the way. And that's the reality.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now you say, what was the man's name that the Wells that brought up Joe Wells, that took care of Joe Wells, what was his name?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: His name was Wells.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, it was his first name?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: The old man. Yeah. I forgot, if I said for certain.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But now you said, did he help bring you up too, the same guy? Me?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Yeah. The old man?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Yeah. Well I know I'm from a different family entirely. Entirely different family. Oh, way, way away. Way away from me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know about them being out here when you came out here?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Well God yes, I knew when they left to come here. Yeah, yeah. It's one of them goodbye when they come to Idaho. Several others too, a lot of them come.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they're coming out here and have anything to do with your landing up here?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Well I don't know. It's exactly the same. Quite a few others had come out here and scattered all over the West in all different places. Very, very different places, as I knew. So things, I grew up in West High School and then went to college two years. So I had some money, made some money. I was in the cafeteria buying young cattle you know, and go away from home 20, 30, 40 miles away. And these young calves were up for a pretty good sight. I'd buy them six or eight at a time, start home with them, and some of them cattlemen would stop me. I'd buy them and I'd sell them. I'd go back and do the same trick right over again. I made some money, but most of them made up their mind they wanted to come West. Well I think so. I just went to see how it looks out West too. So four or five of us made up their mind to come, and by that is what we did.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: So some of them dropped off off in Colorado, others come over here to grow up cold facts. We went to West for about four or five of us, went on to Seattle. We left Oxford, Tennessee, and left Ashford, North Carolina, the 17th of March, 1992. Come up, come to Seattle, and things never could be any worse. We left all that rain in the weather, no jobs at all. All that rain, I got some discusses. We went to visit mine, I was going to find some places different. When I come, I went over here to Spokane, and I was looking at the Pacific map, a really good map. I spoke about Moscone, Julien, and Lewis. I said, God, I know some people. It's all those places. I'm going down there to see if I can find them. I come down here and I found them. Well, I wasn't here very long back. I got a job in the implement store right where the safety was, where the JCPenney. I got a job.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that Shields then? What was the name of the place then, the name of the store?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Well, MJ Shields only. He owned a lot of land around here, over there, too. He had real little electricity plants down there for mark builders, builders, that, that. He had run an itch, you know. Of course, town was very big. But he ran a little electricity plant down there. And here, I worked for him for quite a little while. He got in the carload of coal, and Rayner was just pouring it, but it produced a couple of days. And he needed his own loaders down there. He couldn't find anyone that didn't let Rayner do it. So he was trying one man to do it, help on loaders, and I had to go and help him. I told him, me was going by to work close to that range and do that. And I said, yes. I said, well, you go to hell.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: It's what you do. So I had one way. So I wasn't giving it right now. But I told him, I said, you know, I had room to stay in here, please. I said, you know, I'm going to tell you a little trip. It was just down Portland and see what it looks like.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: He was waiting a time. He was caring. Went to Portland. I think, see, I'm here in Los Angeles. I went down to Los Angeles.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you came back.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Yeah. I stayed down in the fire. And I had friends that lived over in Arizona and went over to the register with them. And I turned around and came back here. And I let my folks know my brother and brother. So they sold out in North Carolina and come here. And we bought a very, very bought a place out here, Moscow Mountain. It's just very out of the prior. It's in the timber, just the edge of the timber. There's a lot of places here. It's for sale. It doesn't matter. But he took a lot of money. And when he didn't have it, well, you couldn't buy a big place here. So we bought that place. And there's an old house on it that I fixed it up to live in for a couple of years. And I went to the bay of the horse and they'd run it. Built a house. I built a house. There were trees where I wanted to build a house.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: And built a good house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you cut any wood to sell in Moscow? A lot of people in the mountain cut wood, didn't they?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Yeah. Yeah, I cut quite a bit of two.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you come into town and sell it?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: And I used that to buy fence posts to put around that 80. And two or three year olds would buy wires that could make fence rounds. And it fixed it up after I built a hole, built a barn. Took out the lead. I got a lot of goddamn money. It was right there, you know, $2 ,200 for that house. And farmers had to clear it up after that van. Got four years later, some old was cleared up and all told. So I might have wanted to sell out. So I sold out. I was coming down here and I was showing down the street. Herman Wilson was running the little grocery store over there. And you're across the street over there. By the big dragger store over there. So he told me he'd bought a little plant down here on West 8th Street and was going into P business among me. I told him I'd come down, I went down there to work for him. Worked for him 19 years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He made a big business out of that, didn't he?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: You're damn north. It's one time he had 125 liver versus work. It's one time working damn nice, you know. Bittering hills, getting in peas and shipping out, you know. Started manufacturing this split peas business, you know. A man got him, told him how. He went into that business, made it, done a lot of packages, you know. Selling all this stuff and selling it all over. So kept that up for a number of years. Fine things went to hell entirely. He dies, you know, and the other fella, the son -in -law tried to run his front and didn't know a damn thing about it. I told folks who went out here and they left California. I was in California to save everybody from hell.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You say you wouldn't live in California to save everybody from hell?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: No, there's nobody in the banks back at the time these three months. That goddamn convolution of all the nationalities outside of hell right there. Makes them feel, it gives them a decent place for a white person to live, whatever.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do in the seed company? What was your job there for all those years?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Well, I give different things, you know. If one person is sick of the place, I take his place and he got another one. I looked at you. I've done so many, many things, you know. Many things. So this year, a friend of the young girl down here in the airport raised so many peas. He had contracted Herman to buy peas from him. He brought in his load. When he did, I took a look and I said, see, you know, my God, Herman could use those sacks. How many of you got at home of that kind?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: It's quite a lot. I said, well, God, Herman could use them again. So I joined Ricks, a young man, a young man, a young man. But he was hurting up everybody. He had any little sacks and he'd got all kinds of sacks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of sacks were they? Were they pea sacks?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Being used again?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Yeah. Used again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Saved money?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Oh, yeah. He got a half price now. People had a price of what the human sources used. They were just as good as you. He hired some of them, had a hold of him, had women passing him. They shipped him out, car loads, extra car loads, two, three, two, three, car loads, at that time. He'd load it up and he'd go out with it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did his business get so big? Was it just that he was the first one to try to do it, or how did he get successful?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Well, his first one started in that way. He done hopefully got done well. Hopefully got done well. Then his whole things finally went to the dogs, you know.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: And this guy was going to say, Tom, are you going to have some specials today? And I says, not what's next. He's going to have pea soup. I went to laugh. He says, what are you laughing about?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: I said, I know the company. Manufacturers have only one company. It does that. And I've worked for him 12 years. Do you think I care a thing about pea soup? He's looking at it now for 12 years. Would you?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: When he says, I'm going after my boss right now. He went after him to come home. He told him about me. He told him my name. He had three other restaurants in Spokane, and he was buying them small packages. Of course it was. He took it up. My name took it up and looked at him here. He bought a whole lot of... several small clothes. He had a carload of them. Yeah, they were from...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Listen, Elmer, I want to ask you a little more about Joe Wells, about Nigger Joe.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, what kind of guy was he? What do you know about him?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Joe Wells, out here in Deary.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Joe the nigger?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Yeah. He went and got married and raised up a bunch of them, you know. And I don't know if maybe some of them would be living yet, for a while. I don't know if so. Track them, if you ask. Matter, you know. For he wasn't even saying to me, you know, I had enough shovels more. I couldn't be fooling with that.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: He said, come out here first. Well, I didn't like Seattle. So I come over to Spokane, and I knew people that lived in Lumis, Washington, you know where that's at?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: I thought, I'd go up in that country and see what that looked like. When I'm at the, on a train, for as it could be, on Wilbur Hartline's Cooley City, for his train went, and I took a bus to the highway from there, over to Brewster, took another bus there, going on up to Conconella and then to Lumis. Well, in the meantime, there's, some of my friends is working up there at Lumis Brewster, at Polk Slat, working on a ranch, and his bachelor there had a North Fine Archer and he did an offer well. So he told them, boys, he got off the tired of bachelor. Didn't they know some young nigga, good working nigga, and his wife, it's hard to come out here and work for him. He says, you're just exactly what he wanted. Told him, his wife was an old and good cook, so forth and so was he, and he worked for us at home quite a lot. Took times along to read and write. He was a good worker. So he, this fellow here wrote to me about, he'd give him $700 a year and pay all expenses out here and so forth for the first year. And he says, it's satisfactory way he raised his ways. So back in South Mexico, that's what they do. I told him to go. I says, all the other niggas around here, there's a real relation. All of them are on starvation.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was Joe Wells you were talking to.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: No, I was talking to another man in town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Another man in town here?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: No, up in Brewster Washington. Oh, I see, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So who was the Negro that he wanted? It wasn't Joe Wells he wanted, it was somebody else, eh?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Well, he wanted this man to come and work for him, you know. These boys gave him my address. Well, I got it. So I asked him what side Beck thought about it. He said he was going to leave it up to me to decide. I said, well, I'll tell you, I'd go. You go out there for a year. If you don't like it, you can come back. I said, I said, you can't do a very good here. Well, he went out there and he liked it. So he got him down well. He got him to follow on the homestead, joined him right there. He had a fine officer. He had been making a lot of money off of it. All right. All right. So anyway.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Here, I'll move this. No, that's all right.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: I'll be out of here in just a minute. Called Brewster's Press. We told him we got about three miles up from here. It's a fortress road. There's Mon Hayes, South of Mary Brewster's Press. That's all I asked. We're going to have a bus going on up. We're going to have to do that junction here. That man got on a bus, you know. There's three seats in that bus. The man and his family is seeing both of them, made them backwards from just hiding them, driving them from. He called him Richardson. I seen these little quinches and I said, well, he went on ahead for a couple of miles from Mon Hayes. I heard the driver called you Richardson. By any chance, you can't be Richardson.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: He asked, is that my name? I said, I had a letter from you one time. I don't know how you remember it right now, no?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: When I'm going to tell you this is one time in your life you're mistaken. I'm a man that's telling him, ever write your letter. When I do, I don't write somebody I don't know, and I don't know you. When I say, well, I'm not through yet talking. I said, Richardson, as I have in mind, you know something about him, he has a colleague man, he's working for him. He said, I'm that man. That's what you want to know. When I said, now I'm going to tell you. After all that talk, I said, don't think you look to me. I'll wait and I'll tell you. Why.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: I'm the one you look to in North Carolina to have hands to come out here and work for you. He said, are you Elmer Wells?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: I said, that's me. He said, thank God. He says, I don't know who to you all right. He said, I'll go over to you, hey, for a few people up in Compton, North Livingston, going up there and see what kind of country does that work. He says, you and I are going up to Lewis, but a hell of a lot. I have to go to Compton, I don't know how my business is. You coming home with me in the morning. They're going to see you in the morning. They took up a homestead.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: He got him to take up a homestead, and he was joining him there, and he painted a land and it leveled. It had a lot of water that was going to waste. They bought a number of sheep and back, went and filed on that homestead, built a house and lived over there and come over and do the cooking for Richardson. He kept on staying on working. They were getting together in a way that was some of the business. He finally got a lot of credit and put out in Orchard. Five or six years, three or four years in Orchard. The blown letters too got in the racket about to mine fans. The cops came out to survey and it took him about one third of Richardson's fine horse. My God, he bought back out. He gave $16 ,000 from his prison. He was a woman of land.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Richardson paid this guy off?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: He came back in the off time and bought him a nice place like Goodman for one year. He right across the schoolhouse and made the church. He came over to his house and did something. He's on it. He got tired of feeding them all. He sold out and came back up there and bought him another place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Back to Brewster?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Back there above Brewster's.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about Joe Wells? Did he do pretty good for himself out by dairy?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: No, not very good. He was getting drunk so often he'd arrest him and get in trouble. He was drunk, raised him and dealt around. He never did do very good. He didn't even want to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, people out there talk about him some still. They remember him because he was the only Negro out there, him and his family. They liked him pretty well. My God, they all liked him. He was truthful and honest by God.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: His dad raised him in order to adopt him. He took him as a slave and raised him. He was a minister and preacher, you know. He always wanted to come west. He sold so much of his land, divided the mountain with him, and they all come together. And as long as it comes, got it everywhere. I know I'm everywhere.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I heard that Joe was really close to the Wells boys, to the other sons. He got along good with him and spent a lot of time with him. Well, God Almighty, yes.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: He sold a lot of his work. Sure, he was raised up with him, with his boys, worked with him right along, come west with him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you think he was pretty well accepted out there then?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: He was, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They didn't care that he was an Negro very much then.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Well they didn't really pay any attention to that.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: I heard he used to get in fights sometimes.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Oh, yes, he would. He'd get drunk, get in a fight. He'd hike out of town, stay out of town for a while, and he wouldn't drink anything about it.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: He's just like some of the whites were doing that way as his head.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I heard he didn't get along with Hayes and Marshall, Aaron, Troy. And I heard that old Benson from Deary was a friend of his too, of Joe Wells's. Yeah, I heard he stuck up for Benson against Marshall, or against Hayes. I don't know whether that's true or not. Right, I don't know much about that.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: I had plenty of trouble with him on making a living.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: And he sold, like a stock, a poor sense of share.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: And he went to $58 a share later and made every one of them millionaires, made two of them millionaires here, you know. Made short billionaires, you know, the old man short, you know. Made him a millionaire. And it's your other fellow. He bought some too and made him a millionaire. But he died in short short, died. So him and the other woman gets married then. Both of them, each one got rich off with that mind, stuff to talk up to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The guy's wife married short, the guy that died, eh?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He must have been real rich then.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Oh, you're damn right. You don't know where he lives, are you?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: You know, the old Mark Miller big home upon North Main, North Polk. They live in the, not just across the street from there, but right across the street from there is where David lived, where David lived. Where it's in the next block right south, they live, first corner house. It's a heck of a block that hard lives in itself. They're a fine home and that's where they both live.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And so he got rich off of Dave's mining stock, eh?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Yes, everybody got it stuck for four cents each.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Four cents.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Right now I just wanted to be able to walk a train coming back from down North Carolina, Georgia. And his contractor, the guy on the train is Bill Wilkins, St. Paul.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who, Days?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Oh, a contractor. A contractor, a days. Been in Napa, St. Paul. He got birthed right by me. So things had gone good, very tough times then.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: So he told his wife he was going to Seattle to see what things looked like. He pulled a round trip ticket. When we got to talking, he said he's leaving on the counter. His health was on the counter of that lake there, you know what I'm saying?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Probably made him happy. I told him Seattle's the worst place to stay this side of hell. He's going to, he's going to, he's going to, he's going to, he's going to, he's going to go. And I just told him back to, to hell with Seattle. So I persuaded him to get off the train in Spokane to me. I told him I knew Spokane was pretty good. And we didn't get off in Spokane. When I got him to get off with me in Spokane, we went to bed together. Next morning, got up and had a meal. So I showed him to Spokane. Pretty good. Finally, I told him let's go by the Chamber of Commerce and find out what the prospectors should build. Well, he told her, he says the prospectors are pretty good. I knew this was a prospectors' good. He's going to build in addition to St. Luke's Hospital. And this man told him about him getting off the train. He says he likes Spokane. He made his mind go to stay there. He said he'd even take a job as a carpenter. But he made his mind he's going to stay in Spokane.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Well, I went back to the hotel, went and ran into the hotel and he called me to the phone. I said, who in the hell wants me?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: And I just got back to the east. Well, I went to the phone and there's a third order at the Chamber of Commerce. I wanted me to come up and bring that man back over there. The contract was over there and I wanted to see him. I went over there and he had a contract. He went to one side and had quite a long talk to her. He showed him his recommendations from the back. Remember walk?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: He hired him. He had the job to build the high school in Kello. He was four when he was sick in St. Luke's Hospital. He was sweating blood and he should be started. So he hired him, saying up on that job $20 a day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he made good, eh?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Yeah, he ran up there and he did.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: He got a credit and the old friend of his life had charge of they had bought some of that stock, you know, mining stock, and they were holding it. And with it, there's the city's money to build that building. He went and bought that stock. It led him a chance. So he better get all of his goods. By the time he got some more, it made him a millionaire.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this Day's stock or somebody else's?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Day's stock.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know Day before he struck it rich?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: There he is.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was he like before that? Was he trying to make money pretty hard?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Well, he had guts and he worked hard. He was trying to make it go out of it. He had to strike that out there and it struck it pretty good, you know.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: By God, just made as much money as he did know what it had to do with it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he have a hunch it was going to be a big strike when he started in on it, or was it just a guess, a wild guess?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: A wild guess. It just started, you know.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: But he noticed he had good order, you know. Actually, he wasn't good. So by God, it turned out as you know how he did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was mining his job before he started in up there?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: I don't know what he did before. Right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There were about 10 of 5 there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: 10 of, yeah. Well, let me see. I know what I wanted to ask you about too was about the Democrats and Republicans in Moscow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, you know Willa Carlson? You know Paul Carlson's wife?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She told me you knew a lot about the old days and politics locally. You know, I guess those, I guess Democrats were pretty weak back in the old days in this county. Yes, they were.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The Republicans run everything, eh?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Yes, they're stronger. Republican has a harsh strategy to have to do it in Missouri.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Well, I've seen, I knew all about the records of the presidents.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Well, I wasn't thinking of the national. I was thinking of the local right here in town and that business.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever see what went on around town here in the politics?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Well, God almighty, I knew what was going on every day.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: All of them running for office and everything else. Chief police and everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, Will told me that you were a good Democrat and you didn't like the Republicans too much.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: I wasn't thinking he was a good Republican. He's alright. But any man that's honest and truthful, that's alright. I don't like the scoundrel. Regardless of what nationality it is.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: I think they're going to call me for a meeting. I'm pretty quick now. It's five o 'clock now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, okay. I thought we had about ten minutes. I got about ten of there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You want to talk for ten minutes more or should we quit now?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Well, I don't know which to say. There's not too much. I knew Mr. Carson, you know. I worked for him for a short time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I thought maybe you could tell me about some of the funny things that you saw happen in politics and town in the old days.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: They used to have five saloons and three whorehouses. Everything was going high, wide, and handsome, you know. One of them was where the depot was at down here on West 8th Street. Right there and then just crossed the streets from it. Another one ended up the street, two boxes, two houses away, three whorehouses. Four flash saloons. The girls in Spokane coming down here doing well and having a hell of a time.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Over at George Creighton, another man here, they liked a couple girls in the well in Spokane. When they come again, for them to hold them and know that somebody else has them, they want them to know. She let them know that they share them. These two come down. She told them what rooms to go to, which one. The only room on the inside turned on his light and it was their own wife laying in bed that I went for.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: She was quit stepping out on their own wives.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Over at Grace Raymond, he was running the place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that teach them a lesson?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Not too much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened to them? Did they get shut down when Prohibition came in?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Only a couple of them before that.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: The city council here passed a law to stop them. But they're still running them over at Pullman. Imagine this preacher here went over there to get them to stop them from improving. Jim quite told them.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: So one of the city council members asked him, how could he tell a woman from the others?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: He said, anybody who has any sense could tell them that. He said, you could smell them two blocks away. He said, he's done one of used perfume. He had one of them used perfume. The others did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did most of the girls in the houses come in from Spokane? Did most of them come in from out of town?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: They come every direction, Pullman.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: They're from Blue Ocean, New York.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever get in there? Those places? Were they nice? Inside?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Oh yeah, they were nice. When I guess I may go, get ready to go to my supper. Okay.
